docs.title: Welcome to MkDocs
docs.text: For full documentation visit mkdocs.org.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
• mkdocs new [dir-name] - Create a new project.
  • mkdocs serve - Start the live-reloading docs server.
  • mkdocs build - Build the documentation site.
  • mkdocs -h - Print help message and exit.

docs.title: Project layout
docs.text:
mkdocs.yml    # The configuration file.
docs/
    index.md  # The documentation homepage.
    ...       # Other markdown pages, images and other files.

docs.title: Tasked to Do
docs.text: • Find the top 3 bad-looking websites and choose one for the UI/UX project. The website will be redesign by us and present to everyone in class later on.

docs.title: How Did We Do It
docs.text:
1. Went on Google and searched for "worst website design".
  2. Looked through several examples and picked three.
  3. Chose one that fits best and easiest for redesign.
  4. Show it to Chris and asking fill question about the project

docs.title: What Website I Chose
docs.text:
I chose the Discount Beds Belfast website.

Here is an image of the website:

docs.title: Why I Chose This
docs.text: • It's look super messy even with a simple design.The design look blank and old. It provided too much information in one page.

docs.title: Are you working alone or team.Why?
docs.text: • I work with my team, because it feel better to share work problems with trusted people.

docs.title: What hope to get out of the classes?
What i learned and did in ACIT 2811 UX/UI last week
7 usability factors
docs.text:
1. Useful - is a product/service useful
  2. Usable - is a product/service usable
  3. Findable - is a product/service findable
  4. Credible - is a product/service credible
  5. Desirable - is a product/service desirable
  6. Accessible - is a product/service accessible
  7. Valuable - is a product/service valuable

docs.title: User-Centered Design
docs.text:
• Context: which the user is using the product or service
  • User Testing: feedback to validate that our assumptions are correct
  • Surveys
  • Interviews

Iterative set of steps to solve problems that are specific to products or services for users

  1. Understand the context of use (how, where, why are people using the product/service)

  2. Specify requirements (what does the product need to do)

  3. Design solutions (to the problems that users are facing)

  4. Evaluate solutions (test, get feedback)

docs.title: Five-stage methodology
docs.text:
1. Empathize - identify problems by people/users

  2. Define - analyzing/defining/articulating the problems from step 1

  3. Ideate - brainstorming, generating as many solutions as possible non-judgemental

  4. Prototype - pick the best solutions and prototype them

  5. Test - use the prototype to validate assumptions and get feedback

docs.title: First activity: Empathize - imagine that you are a prospective student at BCIT, identify a challenge that they might face (different from any challenges that you may have faced)
docs.text:
• Define - one or two specific problems that they would face because of their challenge

  • Ideate - brainstorm a few solutions

  • Prototype - define a possible prototype

  • Test - define how you can test the prototype

docs.title: Second activity: Our group assigned two of these factors, write a brief document that:
docs.text:
1. gives an example of a product/service that DOES fulfill one of the factors, and another product/service that DOES NOT fulfill one of the factors

  2. explain how those products/services could be changed to fulfill or not fulfill your assigned factors

  3. Me and my team chose plastic bottle which is accessible and unvaluable which is everywhere but it not worth much. FOr the other one we pick phone which is accessible and valuable due almost everyone on earth have and using a phone. Group_activity

docs.title: What i learned and did in ACIT 2811 UX/UI last week
Group Activity: Personas
docs.text:
A persona is a description of a possible/ideal user of a product/service. We create personas: * To build empathy * To identify user characteristics * To identify user behaviours * To identify user goals

Create two personas (1 page each) - (photo) - Name - Age - Occupation - Needs & Goals - Pain Points (difficulties)

Group Activity: User Stories

TASK: Personas identify user characteristics, behaviours, and goals User Stories identify features (technical features of a product/service that need to be built)

User Stories make up a list of tasks that you can use to ensure, at the end of the project, that you have built everything you set out to build. For each of your two personas, create three User Stories.

Structure: "As a _, I want to _, so that I can __"

Example: "As a [user], I want to [perform an action], so that I can [achieve a goal]"

As a prospective student, I want to learn about the admission process, so that I can start a program

  • These need to be written from the perspective of your Personas *

Group Activity: User Scenarios

A User Scenarios is a technical description of the steps required to complete one of your user stories

USER STORY As a medical director I want to download spec sheets so that I can verify compliance details

USER SCENARIO 1. Start at the home page 2. click the downloads link in the menu 3. on the downloads page, filter documents 4. click the file link

docs.title: Clarify the 5 Ws
docs.text: Five Ws * Who Who is using the site * What What is the site offering/doing,what should the main focus be * Where Online * When ASAP, later? * Why Why create/redesign the site

docs.title: Feature List
docs.text:
Scope - what are you going to do/implement for this website redesign

e.g. - update branding (colors, typography) - redesign home page layout - redesign ____ page layout - redesign menu

docs.title: Ideation
docs.text:
Generate as many possible solutions to the problem as you can Non-judgemental

Techniques - Brainstorming - Worst Possible Idea - Crazy8s (generate quick sketches (wireframes))

docs.title: Information Architecture (IA)
docs.text:
Organizing, labelling of content - to help users find information

Comprised of several 'systems': - navigation (what type of menu, how many menus, hierarchy) - hierarchy deep (very few top menu links with a lot of links underneath) shallow (many top menu links with very few links underneath) - labelling (what style of language do we use) - content inventory (listing all of the pages/content that the website) - sitemap (a visual representation of all the content) - wireframes (simple line drawings depicting the layout of menus/pages)

docs.title: Group Exercise
docs.text:
As a group, categorize (place) all of the links below into top-level menu headings (3 - 5)

  • Accessibility
  • Audition Info
  • Contact Us
  • Current Shows
  • Donate
  • Getting Here
  • Join Our Team
  • Musical Theatre Camp
  • Our Story
  • Past Shows
  • Ticket Information
  • Venue Information
  • Visitor Questions (FAQ)
  • Volunteering
